author: @lak3d
text: Can i use that 125 volts generator to power my computer (if i make on a big scale 220 volts)

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Your computer has a down-converter, the internal circuitry only uses a few volts, and even that is down-converted from the typically 9-18v from your mains transformer. You don't want to be putting 120-240v directly into your laptop/computer etc.

author: @sirmagnus99
text:
What is the relationship in volts and amp as the speed changes? When its slower, the volts dropped. Did the amps stay the same to equal the energy put in? Do volts and amps both drop with slower speeds? 
It would be interesting if the design set the amps and speed set the volts.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Both drop with rotation speed, its a consequence of the power coming in from the wind equals the power out from the generator.
By changing the number of turns in the windings, you can get more of one, but at the expense/loss of the other, it's still going to be: power OUT equals power IN.

author: @SBDI
text:
Dear Rob.
Explain me this.
A excavator works on hydrolic run on a oil pump pumping oil around.

With this hydrolic we can create tons of power, (newton) if we convert this Newton to kw, regarding calculation the potental electrical output is more than the hydrolic imput.

There are also electric hydrolic at the market, 12vDC 2Ampere with a outupt of 800N, this meens in theory the potential electrical output is bigger than the imput.
Connect the hydrolic to a genarator and you might have more output thab input.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
You are missing the 'leverage' effect using hydraulics. It is correct that you get a huge step up in Newtons', but it is over a shorter distance than the movement of the pumping elements. The hydraulic pump is like the long end of the lever, the output (Newtons) is like the short end of the lever.
The power in equals the power out, minus friction losses in the mechanisms.
Hope that helps.

author: @wheee64
text: Could you share the length (or weight) of the wire you used in both kinds of windings (individual bolts or one winding around the whole thing). I'm curious if the efficiency is similar (voltage generated per length of wire assuming the same drill speed). My thought is that the single coil is less efficient because the south magnets are also putting field through the coil when the norths are on the bolts, whereas in the individually wound bolts, the south field goes outside of the coils.

author: @debstevens-tq5iv
text: I have a question. If you create the small generator, as you have, with the small amount of offset in two halves to eliminate the “stall” if that is a satisfactory term, what if you repeat that on your disc in a larger diameter. Each successive winding would be just enough larger as to not interfere with the previous winding. The holes in the disc would appear in a sort of vortex so the each ring of holes had the necessary offset. More and more magnets would be required for each successive winding. I could imagine 30 rings on each side. That would continually increase the mass of the windings. If each ring required an inch for clearance then the total circumference would be around 60”. If you printed a vey large helical rotor it would take more wind and I would imagine and much larger bearings to eliminate drag. Once it got moving the mass of the windings would act as a flywheel. My concern is this. You mentioned that if the windings around your bolts In the first example had to be very uniform in weight to function properly. Would that also be true in my concentric ring model. If so the the inner rings would have to be thicker and then each successive ring would have to have the same mass as the first. Essentially getting thinner at each ring to maintain the same mass. I could foresee it being an expensive experiment but maybe it would produce more than milliamperes.  Don’t forget that the input like wind or falling water is free.

author: @MarcesAurelius
text:
Does adding a second disc of magnetics on the opposite side of one winding make much difference in to optimize the generator?

It seems it would be harder to turn… stronger field?

and connecting each pair of magnets?
which would now need something like a split drive shaft to exit power for the now entrapped coil… and inelegant brushes?

replies.author: @reverse_massage
replies.text: So long as the 2nd array of magnets are oriented the exact same, there should be a net increase in electrical potential output. However, if you do not get the magnetic fields lined up exactly, you begin to cancel out the magnetic field’s flux, and thereby adversely affecting the potential output. And yes, any increase in electrical potential produces an associated increased back EMF so it would necessarily increase the resistance to turning, but only under increased load.

author: @totherarf
text:
Yet another great and interesting video!

I put it to you that This IS Free Energy!
You get something (for little outlay and no running cost) and the energy source would otherwise be just lost to nature!

Even if a paltry 1% of people who have watched this (5K atm) ..... it would give you 50 people actively thinking of the concepts involved. Even if they never build one themselves those people will talk to others at some point in time and pass on the ideas, like a ripple in a pond. Who knows who you will affect in the future?
Once again ....... Thanks!

replies.author: @NOMAD-qp3dd
replies.text:
Yea I do often think about how we overlook the little things that cumulatively could make a big impact. For example like I make coffee every morning, and that would be cool to ... i dont know have some kind of battery with an outlet that i could carry around the house, for making coffee, or when the kids use a blow dryer, or even some lamps.  And with a few wind turbines charging a few of these kinds of battery packs i could have some in the house being used while others are getting charged, when the ones in the house deplete i flip them out etc.
And if you multiplied that kindof behavior across the population that may be kindof significant, especially if you could scale up to where some of the larger batteries are charged by a little bigger turbines and stationary next to your washer and dryer machine, and multiply that across the population, and that might be some nice reduction in energy pulled from the grid. I suppose it's easy to quantify stuff like that 😂.
He makes me want to build a couple of these and start playing.  because of the geography of where i live there's plenty of wind.  There's plenty of sun too but i kindof don't like the solar panel industry.  I've heard just making one solar panel makes such a carbon footprint it takes years for that solar panel to make it back, and it could fail to work very well before it makes it back.
But wind is solid.  All ya need is grease and lube for the spinny parts. 😂

author: @SimonPlatten
text:
Hi Rob, can you explain the purpose of the heat shrink as I was originally thinking it was to insult and prevent the coil from being in contact with the bolt...however do the washers bridge between the coil and the bolt removing the insulation the heat shrink provides?  What is the specification of the copper wire used?

Another suggestion, most of the projects directly drive the generator, could you instead drive a large wheel with a belt to drive the generator, 1 turn of the large wheel would be several or many turns of the generator and hence a lot more output ?

replies.author: @MushInSkull
replies.text: The threads are sharp and the shrink wrap is to prevent the threads from cutting into the enameled surface of the magnet wire. It's not to insulate and protect the wire from being in contact with the bolt. The wire has  an enamel coating that insulates it. If the wire didn't have an enamel coating, the wire coil would short circuit and instead of thousands of turns, it would be one.

author: @jasonmorello1374
text: I am thinking that this looks like a prime candidate for a windwall setup. one of these at the top and bottom of spindle axles going thru the vertical wind you want, going say 6 feet tall, 8 inches wide, times maybe 10, and another wall at 90 degrees if the wind is very irregular where you set it up, and carry up the leads of the generators to 16 or 12 gage electricians wire. take that over to your regulator set up and storage. I personally am looking at a mixed storage of hydrogen(via metal hydride flasks) and a standard battery bank system, so the battery levels out most of the flow, and the hydrogen goes to some burning engine for long down times.

author: @RupertBruce
text: I'm surprised you didn't leap on the serpentine arrangement with the copper weaving towards an inner ring of bolts. I like the carriage bolt improvement. I am curious to know if a shorter bolt would reduce the output significantly... What is the perfect bolt length? What if you did concentric rings at different voltages? I agree that wind is a necessary component of a diverse energy portfolio.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
A shorter bolt does not have to equal less output.
Keep in mind that it is about the length of the wire.

If you put shorter bolts with larger OD washers that enable you to wind the same length of wire onto the shorter bolts, you would get the same output.

replies.author: @reverse_massage
replies.text: Shorter bolts with a fatter wind may actually be beneficial because more of the wire length would ultimately make and break contact with the magnetic field. There’s no way the bottoms of those tall coils are within the magnetic fields of those tiny magnets no matter how strong they may be…

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@reverse_massage 

That's not how it works.

This set up uses to bolts to bend the magnetic field so that it is moving across the wire in a way that enables the generation of electricity.

The magnetic field transfers into the bolts making the bolt temporarily transforming the bolt into a magnet.

When the magnet moves away from the bolt and the next magnet which is set opposite (north south north south), it changes the direction of the magnetic field in the bolt. 

As a result, the coil is within the magnetic field of the bolt and that magnetic field is shifting from north to south thereby creating an alternating current.

If the magnet's field was not transferring into the bolt, the device would not generate electricity because the copper wire would not be moving at a right angle through the magnetic field properly.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: @scotttovey   I'm still very much a newbie with all this stuff, but was wondering based on what you wrote, would using a more highly magnetic bolt, like a more iron rich metal, work better than the stainless steel that is being used?

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@justinw1765 

"would using a more highly magnetic bolt, like a more iron rich metal, work better than the stainless steel that is being used?"

First, I don't think Robert would have used stainless steel. He would have went with the standard off the shelve bolts that are more ferrous than stainless steel.

It is a good bet that using iron bolts or rods, will result in stronger current generation as a result of there being less resistance to the magnets magnetic flux. 
How much of an improvement can only be determined through experimenting.

author: @velcroman11
text: You could use a step down transformer say 2:1 primary : secondary.  The voltage output of the trans former will half the voltage of the input and the out put current will be doubled.  Remember power = voltage times current.

author: @velcroman11
text: That wire surrounding the entire set of steel bolts works because the magnetic field from the magnets puts a varying magnetic field into the bolts.  Replacing the steel bolts with wooden dowel and it won’t work.

author: @jaedy1124
text:
As someone who has been living off-grid, in the middle of nowhere for 4 years in harsh northern winters, Solar has been vastly more reliable and cost-effective than wind by far. 
Here is why: I have two wind generators. I paid $400 on the low end and $800 on the high end. I have a VAWT and a HAWT. Both from companies out of China, both highly recommended (by people who obviously don't need to rely on them to light their home or charge their phone). The HAWT was rated for 350 Watts, the VAWT was rated for 1200 Watts. Neither of them have made an appreciable difference to my charge levels even in the highest winds. My reason for getting them was to compensate for the extremely limited sun in the winter. We live far enough north that the sun is very low over the horizon in the winters, usually with heavy cloud cover and snow. And yet, even with those conditions, Bi-facial Solar panels have wiped the floor with wind. We live on the edge of an 8 acre open field which means we see some steady, and sometimes quite high winds, yet store bought generators have never returned our investment. 
So while I cannot whip up a solar panel in my barn, they have paid us back 10-fold when compared to the wind generators we have purchased so far. 
Now, with all of that said, something that would help real people who rely on renewables for ALL their power needs (no generator), creating a generator that can produce enough power in a reasonable amount of wind to raise the charge of a 24V/800AH battery bank would be something I would watch with rapt attention. I agree in principle that home-made wind generators are the way to go, but they MUST justify the time/materials spent on them by providing enough power to make any real difference. Otherwise, they are little more than fun toys.

author: @awesomedee5421
text: Maybe I missed it, but does the thickness of the enamelled copper wire make a difference or any gauge will work?

replies.author: @reverse_massage
replies.text: It makes a difference in that the thicker the wire is, the less length you’ll be able to shove into a given winding. So thinner gauge wire tends to give you a larger voltage potential. Of course ultimately less amperage could be handled by a thinner wire but as he’s already stated, high amperage will never be an issue with this design.

replies.author: @DynamicMindTribe
replies.text: Hello, just an un educated question, when creating generators such as this why isn’t there use of a way to step up the output to a higher level?

replies.author: @mememan2344
replies.text:
@DynamicMindTribe it's because it's just one part in a system. You are correct, this is where things like joule theifs and step up converters come in. When you're building your own powerplant, you have to handle literally every part of the process. Rob is just showing us the millions of ways you can get that electric current flowing out of whatever you have, be it heat, wind, water, etc (what he calls your prime mover).

You might need to connect it to a gearbox to make it spin faster and generate higher voltage, maybe you choose another coil and magnet pattern, etc. This is the world of the Maker, you can do anything.

You'll have to customize your build to your needs, and the advantage is that you'll save a ton of money making your own powerplants, vs paying a minimum of thousands for a generator coming from commercial sources.

25 volts 100 milliamps as shown in the video isn't bad. You could just stick a buck boost converter on there, luckily as long as you have a couple watts at least, you can convert it using off the shelf components for pretty cheap.
